questions.question: Define sound and explain how it is produced.
questions.answer: Sound is a form of energy that is produced due to vibration of an object. When an object vibrates, it causes the surrounding particles to vibrate, producing sound waves.

questions.question: Explain the difference between longitudinal and transverse waves with examples.
questions.answer: Longitudinal waves: particles vibrate parallel to direction of wave propagation (e.g., sound). Transverse waves: particles vibrate perpendicular to wave propagation (e.g., light, water surface).

questions.question: Explain the terms wavelength, frequency, and amplitude of a sound wave.
questions.answer: Wavelength: distance between two consecutive crests or compressions. Frequency: number of vibrations per second (Hz). Amplitude: maximum displacement of particles from mean position, related to loudness.

questions.question: What is the speed of sound in air? How does temperature affect it?
questions.answer: Speed of sound in air at 0°C ≈ 331 m/s. Speed increases with temperature: v = 331 + 0.6T (T in °C).

questions.question: Explain echo and its practical uses.
questions.answer: Echo is the reflection of sound from a surface. Used in sonar, bats' navigation, echo-sounding in submarines.

questions.question: Explain the term ‘ultrasound’ and its applications.
questions.answer: Ultrasound are sound waves of frequency above 20 kHz, not audible to humans. Applications: medical imaging (sonography), cleaning objects, detecting flaws in materials.

questions.question: What is the difference between noise and musical sound?
questions.answer: Noise: unpleasant, irregular vibrations, non-periodic, unpredictable (e.g., traffic sound). Musical sound: pleasant, periodic vibrations, definite pitch (e.g., flute, guitar).

questions.question: Explain the phenomenon of resonance with an example.
questions.answer: Resonance occurs when the frequency of an external force matches the natural frequency of an object, causing large amplitude vibrations. Example: tuning fork making another fork vibrate.

questions.question: Explain how sound travels in solids, liquids, and gases.
questions.answer: Sound travels by vibrating particles. Speed is highest in solids, medium in liquids, and lowest in gases due to particle density and elasticity.

questions.question: A tuning fork produces a sound of frequency 512 Hz. Calculate its time period.
questions.answer: Time period T = 1/f = 1/512 ≈ 0.00195 s

questions.question: Explain loudness and pitch of sound.
questions.answer: Loudness: perception of intensity of sound, depends on amplitude. Pitch: perception of frequency, high frequency → high pitch, low frequency → low pitch.

questions.question: Explain why sound cannot travel in vacuum.
questions.answer: Sound requires a medium to vibrate. In vacuum, there are no particles, so sound cannot propagate.

questions.question: Explain the working of SONAR using sound waves.
questions.answer: SONAR emits ultrasound waves. Waves reflect from underwater objects. Time taken for echo determines distance. Used in submarines and detecting underwater objects.

questions.question: Explain the difference between infrasound and ultrasound.
questions.answer: Infrasound: frequency below 20 Hz, used in earthquake detection, animal communication. Ultrasound: frequency above 20 kHz, used in medical imaging, cleaning.

questions.question: Explain the principle of sound reflection and its applications.
questions.answer: Sound reflection occurs when sound waves hit a surface and bounce back. Applications: echo, sonar, architectural acoustics.

questions.question: Explain why sound travels faster in solids than in air.
questions.answer: Particles in solids are closer and more elastic than in air, allowing faster transfer of vibrations → higher speed of sound.

questions.question: Explain reverberation and how it can be reduced in halls.
questions.answer: Reverberation: prolongation of sound due to multiple reflections. Reduced by using sound-absorbing materials like curtains, carpets, acoustic panels.

questions.question: Explain how humans produce and perceive sound.
questions.answer: Produced by vocal cord vibrations, transmitted through air. Perceived by ear: sound waves vibrate eardrum → ossicles → cochlea → auditory nerve → brain interprets.

questions.question: Explain how musical instruments produce sound of different pitch.
questions.answer: Pitch depends on frequency of vibration. Shorter strings or smaller air columns vibrate faster → higher pitch. Longer or thicker → lower pitch.

questions.question: A sound wave has frequency 500 Hz. Find its time period.
Explain the difference between loudness and intensity of sound.
questions.answer: Loudness: human perception of sound strength. Intensity: energy transported per unit area per second, physical quantity. Loudness depends on intensity and sensitivity of ear.

questions.question: Explain why the frequency of sound determines its pitch.
questions.answer: Higher frequency → particles vibrate faster → higher pitch. Lower frequency → slower vibration → lower pitch.
answer_outline: Relation of frequency and particle vibration

questions.question: Explain why sound waves are longitudinal waves.
questions.answer: In sound waves, particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the direction of wave propagation, which is the definition of longitudinal waves.

questions.question: Explain the working of a stethoscope.
questions.answer: Stethoscope channels sound from patient’s body through air-filled tubes to listener’s ear. Amplifies heart and lung sounds.

questions.question: Explain the factors affecting speed of sound in a medium.
questions.answer: Depends on medium’s density and elasticity. Higher elasticity → higher speed. Higher density → lower speed. Temperature increase also increases speed in gases.
